era_context: The mid-15th century was a period of significant political consolidation and transition in Europe and Asia. The Ottoman Empire was expanding aggressively, the Italian Renaissance was flourishing, the Hundred Years' War was in its final stages, and the Ming Dynasty maintained stability in China. This was a transitional period between medieval and early modern political structures, marked by the rise of centralized nation-states and the decline of feudal systems.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Interior Americas (Aztec and Inca records fragmentary)
Central Asia and Steppe regions (limited documentation)

events.title: First Battle of St. Albans (Wars of the Roses begin)
events.description: The First Battle of St. Albans in 1455 marked the beginning of the Wars of the Roses, a series of civil conflicts between the Houses of York and Lancaster for control of the English throne. This battle was fought between the Duke of Somerset and the Duke of York, with York's forces emerging victorious. The Wars of the Roses would continue for 30 years and significantly weaken English power, contributing to the end of the Hundred Years' War.
events.certainty_note: The First Battle of St. Albans is well-documented in English records. The date is certain.
cross_references: Led to 1485-001 (Battle of Bosworth Field, end of Wars of the Roses)

events.title: Ottoman-Venetian tensions in the Mediterranean
events.description: Tensions between the Ottoman Empire and Venice were escalating in 1455, as the Ottomans sought to consolidate control over Mediterranean trade routes and island territories. These tensions would erupt into open war in 1463 (First Venetian-Ottoman War). The conflict represented the broader struggle between the declining Venetian maritime republic and the rising Ottoman Empire.
key_figures: Venetian Senate and merchant republic leadership

sources.name: Venetian state archives and diplomatic correspondence

events.certainty_note: Pre-war tensions are documented in Venetian records, though specific incidents in 1455 are not always precisely dated.

events.title: Afonso V of Portugal campaigns in North Africa
events.description: Afonso V of Portugal, known as 'the African,' continued Portuguese military campaigns in North Africa, seeking to expand Portuguese influence and promote Christian crusading ideals. These campaigns were part of the broader Portuguese Age of Exploration and the Reconquista's extension into Africa. Portuguese expansion in North Africa would continue through the 1460s and beyond.

sources.name: Gomes Eanes de Zurara, Chronicle of the Discovery and Conquest of Guinea

events.certainty_note: Portuguese campaigns in North Africa during this period are documented, though specific operations in 1455 are not always precisely dated.
cross_references: Part of broader Portuguese Age of Exploration

events.title: Ming Dynasty stability under Emperor Zhengtong
events.description: In 1455, the Ming Dynasty was experiencing relative stability under Emperor Zhengtong (who had been restored to power in 1457, but was still in power in 1455 as Jingtai's successor). The Ming court was consolidating power and managing threats from Mongol incursions on the northern frontier. This period saw the continuation of Ming cultural and economic development.
events.certainty_note: The Ming Dynasty's stability during this period is documented in Ming records, though specific events in 1455 are not always precisely dated.

events.title: Ashikaga shogunate fragmentation in Japan
events.description: The Ashikaga shogunate continued to experience fragmentation and loss of central authority in 1455. Competing daimyo factions were consolidating regional power, and the shogun's authority was increasingly challenged. This period of decentralization would eventually lead to the Sengoku (Warring States) period, which began in 1467 with the Onin War.

sources.name: Onin War chronicles and later Japanese historical compilations

events.certainty_note: The general political fragmentation of this period is well-documented, though specific events within 1455 are not always precisely dated.

disconfirming_evidence: The First Battle of St. Albans is sometimes dated to May 22, 1455, but some sources give slightly different dates. The battle's significance as the 'beginning' of the Wars of the Roses is somewhat conventional; some scholars argue that the conflict had deeper roots in the 1450s or earlier. The Ming Dynasty's political situation in 1455 is complex; Emperor Zhengtong had been deposed in 1449 and was succeeded by Jingtai, so 1455 was during Jingtai's reign, not Zhengtong's. This can cause confusion in secondary sources.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1455 is rich in European sources, particularly from England, Portugal, Venice, and the Ottoman Empire. English sources on the Wars of the Roses are abundant but often biased toward the victorious Tudor dynasty. Chinese sources are abundant but focus on court-centered events. Japanese sources are available but often later compilations. African and American sources are virtually absent from the surviving written record. The bias toward political and military events is pronounced; economic, cultural, and social developments are less systematically recorded. Ottoman sources are often later compilations rather than contemporary records.

graph_edges.note: The First Battle of St. Albans initiated the Wars of the Roses, which ended with the Battle of Bosworth Field in 1485.
Escalating Ottoman-Venetian tensions erupted into open war in 1463.
Portuguese campaigns in North Africa continued and expanded, leading to the conquest of Tangier in 1471.
The Ming Dynasty's stability during this period led to the restoration of Zhengtong in 1457.
The political fragmentation of the Ashikaga shogunate led to the Onin War in 1467.

_meta.note: Produced via Claude Code CLI (Max subscription) before API migration
